description:
You are helping a data-analytics colleague who has given you a small list of Internet domains in a CSV file and needs their corresponding IPv4 addresses for further processing.

Initial setup  
• A file named /home/user/data/domains.csv already exists.  
• Its exact contents are:  
  domain  
  example.com  
  iana.org  

Goal  
1. For every domain in /home/user/data/domains.csv, determine the first (primary) IPv4 “A” record that the system’s resolver returns.  
2. Create a new CSV file at /home/user/output/resolved_domains.csv that contains those mappings.

Required output format (must match exactly, including header, order and the final newline):  
domain,ipv4  
example.com,93.184.216.34  
iana.org,192.0.43.8  

Constraints & hints  
• Use only standard Linux command-line tools available in a minimal distribution (e.g., getent, dig, host, awk, cut, etc.).  
• The file /home/user/output/resolved_domains.csv must be created by your commands; overwrite if it already exists.  
• Keep the column order exactly as shown (“domain” first, “ipv4” second) and ensure the file ends with a single trailing newline so that automated tests can read the final line properly.  
• No extra spaces, tabs, or quotes around the commas or values.  

Once finished, double-check that /home/user/output/resolved_domains.csv matches the format above byte-for-byte.
truth:
Pre-existing files and directories  
1. Directory /home/user/data/ (permissions 755)  
2. File /home/user/data/domains.csv (permissions 644) with the exact three lines, ending in a single newline:  
domain  
example.com  
iana.org  

3. Directory /home/user/output/ (permissions 755) – initially empty.

Expected artefact created by the agent  
File: /home/user/output/resolved_domains.csv (permissions 644)  
Exact body (must end with \n):  
domain,ipv4  
example.com,93.184.216.34  
iana.org,192.0.43.8  

The test harness will open /home/user/output/resolved_domains.csv and compare its content byte-for-byte to the text above.
